findings.message: N+1 query pattern: get_item_names first selects all Item.id values, then issues a separate SELECT per id via session.get(Item, iid) inside the loop. On an unbounded items table this is O(N) round-trips per request — a clear performance regression for an endpoint that only needs the name column. Latency and DB connection-pool pressure grow linearly with row count.
findings.suggestion: Fetch names in a single query, e.g. `names = list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id)))`. This drops the endpoint to one round-trip and avoids loading full ORM objects. Also consider adding pagination (LIMIT/OFFSET or keyset) since the result set is unbounded.

findings.message: Unbounded result set: get_item_names materializes every row's name into a Python list with no LIMIT/pagination. Combined with the N+1 above, a large items table can exhaust the connection's memory and the request worker, and amplifies pool-exhaustion risk under concurrency.
findings.suggestion: Add pagination (e.g. `limit`/`offset` or keyset query parameters) and apply a server-side cap before returning, so a single request cannot fan out to arbitrarily many DB calls or allocations.

findings.message: names.append in a tight per-row loop is unnecessary allocation in the hot path when the query can yield the strings directly. Minor on its own, but combined with the N+1 it compounds per-request CPU/GC cost.
findings.suggestion: Replace the loop with a single `list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id)))` — eliminates both the per-iteration append and the per-row ORM materialization.
